item.title: Fugitive who stole identity of college grad pleads guilty to fraud
item.description: A fugitive who lived for more than 40 years under the stolen identity of a University of Arkansas graduate has pleaded guilty to fraud, among other charges.

item.title: What assets do creditors target after winning a debt lawsuit?
item.description: A court judgment can open the door to more than collection calls, so it's important to know what may be at risk.

item.title: Senior Iranian officer says return to war with U.S. seems "inevitable"
item.description: Prospects for a U.S.-Iran deal seem to dim as the war between Israel and Hezbollah grinds on despite Trump saying they agreed to stop fighting.

item.title: Trump administration backs off on "anti-weaponization" fund amid backlash
item.description: The Trump administration says it will stop work on the Justice Department's nearly $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization fund" amid backlash from both Republicans and Democrats. CBS News' Kathryn Watson has more.

item.title: Marco Rubio testifies on Capitol Hill, says U.S. is "not a charity" and "we are here to win"
item.description: State Secretary Marco Rubio is facing lawmakers on Capitol Hill to make the case for his department's 2027 budget. During his opening statement, Rubio touted his efforts with the Trump administration, reiterated the recent U.S. shifts on foreign aid and discussed negotiations with Iran.

item.title: Maine voters react to Graham Platner scandals as Bernie Sanders slams distractions
item.description: Some Maine voters backing Graham Platner's Democratic bid for U.S. Senate don't appear to be dissuaded by the latest scandals plaguing his campaign. CBS News' Caitlin Huey-Burns reports.

item.title: Tech companies are facing reckoning over AI spending
item.description: Big tech companies that invested in artificial intelligence are grappling with its rising costs. Emily Bary, an assistant managing editor for tech at MarketWatch, joins CBS News with more.

item.title: Trump names controversial housing official Bill Pulte as acting intel chief
item.description: President Trump on Tuesday announced he's tapping housing official Bill Pulte to serve as the acting director of national intelligence to replace Tulsi Gabbard.

item.title: 6 states hold primaries today, including key races in California, Iowa
item.description: Six states are holding primary contests on Tuesday, including California and Iowa.

item.title: Legendary wine unveiled after decades hidden under castle floor
item.description: The collection once belonged to the noble Beaufort-Spontin family, who were suspected of having collaborated with the Nazis.

item.title: P!nk previews "so many surprises" and top moments as she prepares to host Tony Awards
item.description: Grammy Award winner P!nk is preparing to host the Tony Awards on Sunday on CBS. The singer speaks with "CBS Mornings" about what to expect and why she asked her daughter for permission before agreeing to host.

item.title: Iowa voters enter primaries with concerns about the economy, farmers' futures
item.description: Iowa is holding key primaries for the gubernatorial and U.S. Senate races as economic woes continue to plague farmers and others in the state. CBS News' Lana Zak reports.

item.title: Trump pausing DOJ "anti-weaponization" fund that Kelly slammed as "corruption in broad daylight"
item.description: The Trump administration is halting the "anti-weaponization" fund that got bipartisan backlash and an unfavorable ruling in court. CBS News' Weijia Jiang reports.

item.title: Deadly protests in Kenya over U.S. facility to contain Ebola
item.description: Protesters clashed in Kenya over a U.S. plan to install a facility there to contain the Ebola outbreak. CBS News' Ramy Inocencio reports.

item.title: WHO massively scales back number of suspected Ebola cases in Congo
item.description: While 330 Ebola infections are confirmed in central Africa and huge challenges remain, hundreds more suspected cases "have been cleared out," the WHO says.

item.title: Deadly Russian strikes slam Ukraine, survivor describes blast and being trapped in rubble
item.description: Russian strikes overnight across Ukraine killed at least 13 people, officials say, but the death toll is expected to rise as responders seek people trapped in rubble. CBS News' Aidan Stretch reports from Kyiv.

item.title: Exclusive discounts from CBS Mornings Deals
item.description: On this edition of CBS Mornings Deals, we show you items that will help improve your everyday lifestyle. Visit cbsdeals.com to take advantage of these exclusive deals today. CBS earns commissions on purchases made through cbsdeals.com.

item.title: Russian missile, drone attack on Ukraine kills at least 13, officials say
item.description: Russian missile, drone strikes kill at least 13 people across Ukraine, authorities say, after President Zelenskyy warned Moscow was planning a "massive new strike."

item.title: Behind the decline of summer jobs for teens
item.description: A new report finds summer hiring for teens is expected to fall to its lowest level in nearly 80 years. Harvard economist and CBS News contributor Roland Fryer explains what's driving the decline.

item.title: Israel and Lebanon are clashing despite Trump promising a pause in fighting to soothe Iran tensions
item.description: Israel and Lebanon clashed overnight despite President Trump claiming that both sides would pause fighting. CBS News' Courtney Kealy reports.

item.title: Breaking down findings of new study on weight loss drugs and breast cancer
item.description: Researchers from Penn Medicine found women taking GLP-1 drugs are less likely to be diagnosed with breast cancer. Dr. Celine Gounder breaks down the study's findings and if the drugs caused the difference.

item.title: More than a dozen killed in major Russian attack on Ukraine
item.description: Dozens of missiles and drones hit Kyiv and other major cities in Ukraine on Tuesday. More than a dozen people were killed and 100 injured in the attacks. Russia says it was in response to what it called "terrorist acts." Ramy Inocencio has the latest.

item.title: Ebola outbreak may have been spreading since early January
item.description: The International Rescue Committee says the current Ebola outbreak in central Africa may have been spreading undetected as early as January. Meanwhile, there are protests over U.S. plans to treat affected Americans in Kenya. Ramy Inocencio reports.

item.title: American soldier and British soldier die in training accident in Iraq
item.description: The U.S. and British militaries say one American soldier and one British soldier died during a training exercise in Iraq.

item.title: Bear goes on rampage in Japan, injuring 4 people
item.description: A record 13 people were killed by bears in Japan last year, and there has been a jump in sightings as the animals emerge hungry from hibernation.

item.title: Ancient coin, mysterious markings found under Notre Dame cathedral
item.description: A team of archaeologists at the iconic cathedral is digging straight down and back in time, to Roman Paris 2,000 years ago.

item.title: Police in Iowa say they think man shot and killed 6 relatives, then himself
item.description: Authorities in Iowa are investigating the fatal shootings of six people they believe were killed by a relative who took his own life when confronted by police.

item.title: Northlake police warn of gas pump screw scam
item.description: The Northlake Police Department issued a warning about a new scam at gas stations that is low tech, but effective.

item.title: Bus driver in Virginia crash that killed 5 facing more charges
item.description: Jing Sheng Dong, a 48-year-old tour bus driver from Staten Island, New York, faces three additional felony counts in connection with the deaths.

item.title: U.S. political commentators say U.K. barred them "for criticizing Israel"
item.description: Left-wing streamers Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker say Britain's government has denied them entry over their criticism of Israel.
